content_markdown:
When an electric cell of negligible internal resistance is connected to a resistor of resistance $4R$, the power dissipated in the resistor is $P$.

What is the power dissipated in a resistor of resistance value $R$ when it is connected to the same cell?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Power dissipated in a resistor connected to a constant voltage source $V$ is given by $P = \frac{V^2}{R_{total}}$. Initially, $P = \frac{V^2}{4R}$. When the resistance is changed to $R$, the new power $P'$ is $\frac{V^2}{R}$. Comparing the two, $P' = 4 \times \left(\frac{V^2}{4R}\right) = 4P$. Since the internal resistance is negligible, the terminal voltage remains constant.
